concept_name: The Bar Magnet as a Magnetic Dipole — closed-loop field, no monopole, m, 1/r³
source_book: NCERT Class 12 Physics Part 1, Chapter 5 (Magnetism and Matter) §5.2 (The Bar Magnet) — table-of-contents reference only; teaching authored from first principles. The atomic concept taught here is what a bar magnet IS: a MAGNETIC DIPOLE. Its magnetic field lines are continuous CLOSED loops — they emerge from the N pole, curve around outside to the S pole, and continue INSIDE the magnet from S back to N, so a line never starts or ends (unlike electric field lines, which begin on + charge and end on − charge). A direct consequence is that there is NO isolated magnetic pole (no magnetic monopole): break a bar magnet and you get two complete smaller magnets, each with its own N and S — new poles appear at the cut. The bar magnet carries a magnetic moment m that points from S to N, and it is the EQUIVALENT of a solenoid of moment m = NIA, producing the same far field. That far field is the dipole field, falling as 1/r³: at the same distance r the AXIAL field (along the magnet's axis) is exactly TWICE the EQUATORIAL field (perpendicular to it) — B_axial = (μ₀/4π)(2m/r³), B_equatorial = (μ₀/4π)(m/r³). This is the exact electrostatic ANALOG of an electric dipole (m ↔ p, μ₀/4π ↔ 1/4πε₀). The deepest student confusions targeted: (M1) field lines start at N and end at S like charges; (M2) you can isolate a north pole by cutting the magnet; (M3) magnetism is unrelated to electric current; (M4) the axial and equatorial fields are equal; (M5) the dipole field falls as 1/r² like a point charge. Four of these (M1, M2, M4, M5) are confronted at explicit misconception_watch pivot states via a straightforward cause-then-effect contrast beat (Rule 31, 2026-07-02 — never a predict-pause); M3 is taught straightforwardly with no watch entry. CUT-LINE: this concept teaches the bar magnet's IDENTITY and FIELD only — it does NOT cover the torque/oscillation/energy of a bar magnet in an external uniform field (τ = m × B, U = −m·B), which is the sibling concept bar_magnet_in_uniform_field, nor Gauss's law for magnetism (∮B·dA = 0), which is gauss_law_magnetism. Conceptual-only ship (no mode_overrides). EPIC-L-first (no epic_c_branches).

real_world_anchor.primary: Picture the red-and-blue bar magnet from your school physics lab, the one you slid under a sheet of paper sprinkled with iron filings — the filings snapped into curved lines arcing from one end to the other. Those curves are exactly the field you will see here, and this concept finally explains why they form closed loops and why you can never shake out a single magnetic pole.
real_world_anchor.secondary: Picture the small magnetic compass needle used in your geography or physics class. That needle is itself a tiny bar magnet — a magnetic dipole — and it lines up tangent to the very field lines drawn here. Its red end is the magnet's N, pointing along the field.
real_world_anchor.tertiary: Picture snapping a fridge magnet or a magnetic strip in half. You might expect one piece to keep the north and the other the south — but each broken piece is instantly a complete magnet again, with a fresh N and S at the break. That stubborn fact is the no-monopole rule.

m.name: Magnetic moment of the bar magnet

formulas.field_lines_closed: Magnetic field lines form continuous CLOSED loops: out of N, around outside to S, and through the magnet from S back to N — never starting or ending.
formulas.no_monopole: No isolated magnetic pole exists: cut a bar magnet and each piece is a complete dipole, with a new N and S appearing at the cut.
formulas.moment: m = N I A — the bar magnet is equivalent to a solenoid of N turns, current I, area A; m points from S to N.
formulas.axial_field: B_axial = (mu0 / 4pi) * (2 m / r^3) — on the axis, distance r >> magnet length.
formulas.equatorial_field: B_equatorial = (mu0 / 4pi) * (m / r^3) — perpendicular to the axis, same r.
formulas.axial_twice_equatorial: B_axial = 2 * B_equatorial at the same distance r; both fall as 1 / r^3 (a dipole field, NOT 1/r^2).
formulas.electrostatic_analog: Exact analog of an electric dipole: m <-> p, mu0/4pi <-> 1/(4pi*eps0); the field patterns are identical.

constraints: Magnetic field lines are continuous and closed - they have no start or end point, unlike electric field lines which begin on positive charge and end on negative charge.
There is no magnetic monopole: breaking a magnet always yields two dipoles, never an isolated N or S.
The magnetic moment m points from the S pole to the N pole, with magnitude m = N I A (the equivalent solenoid).
The dipole far field falls as 1/r^3 (not 1/r^2): on the axis B = (mu0/4pi)(2m/r^3), on the equatorial line B = (mu0/4pi)(m/r^3).
At equal distance r the axial field is exactly TWICE the equatorial field, and the two point in opposite senses relative to m (axial along m, equatorial opposite to m).
The bar magnet is the exact magnetic analog of an electric dipole (m plays the role of p, mu0/4pi plays the role of 1/4pi*eps0).

aha_moment.statement: Break a bar magnet and you never get a lone pole — only two dipoles.
aha_moment.visual_confirmation: STATE_3 splits the magnet at its centre; brand-new N and S poles ignite at the cut, each half re-closes its own field loop, then a second cut elsewhere repeats the same result.

STATE_1.title: A bar magnet's field — a compass settles tangent to it

scene_composition.text: A bar magnet (N red, S blue) and its field

Green field lines B loop out of N and
back into S; amber dots drift steadily
along them, showing the field's direction.

A compass needle settles and lines up
tangent to that same field direction —
just like the needle in your classroom.
A bar magnet is a source of a directional magnetic field B

tts_sentences.text_en: A bar magnet's field forms closed loops, with amber dots drifting steadily from the north pole around to the south.
tts_sentences.text_hi: एक bar magnet का field closed loops बनाता है, amber dots उत्तर वाले pole से दक्षिण वाले pole तक लगातार बहते हैं।
tts_sentences.text_te: ఒక bar magnet యొక్క field closed loops ఏర్పరుస్తుంది, amber dots north pole నుండి south pole వరకు స్థిరంగా కదులుతాయి.

tts_sentences.text_en: A moment later, a compass needle settles and lines up exactly along that same direction.
tts_sentences.text_hi: थोड़ी देर बाद, एक compass needle settle होती है और ठीक उसी direction में align हो जाती है।
tts_sentences.text_te: కొద్ది సేపటి తర్వాత, ఒక compass needle settle అయ్యి, సరిగ్గా అదే direction లో align అవుతుంది.

tts_sentences.text_en: So a bar magnet is really a source of a directional magnetic field B, not just a glowing object.
tts_sentences.text_hi: तो एक bar magnet असल में एक directional magnetic field B का source है, सिर्फ चमकती हुई वस्तु नहीं।
tts_sentences.text_te: కాబట్టి ఒక bar magnet నిజంగా ఒక directional magnetic field B యొక్క source, కేవలం మెరిసే వస్తువు కాదు.

STATE_2.title: The field line is a CLOSED loop — it never ends at S

scene_composition.text: Follow ONE field line all the way around

A faint tracer rides the outside arc from
N toward S and appears to just die out
at the south pole.

A brighter tracer keeps going, straight
through the magnet from S back to N —
the line closes on itself.
Magnetic field lines are CLOSED loops — electric field lines start and end on charge

tts_sentences.text_en: Follow one field line: it leaves the north pole, arcs outside, and looks like it stops at the south.
tts_sentences.text_hi: एक field line को follow कीजिए: यह north pole से निकलती है, बाहर arc करती है, और south पर रुकती हुई लगती है।
tts_sentences.text_te: ఒక field line ను follow చేయండి: ఇది north pole నుండి బయలుదేరి, బయట arc అయ్యి, south వద్ద ఆగినట్టు కనిపిస్తుంది.

tts_sentences.text_en: Watch closely — a brighter dot keeps going, right through the magnet, back from south to north.
tts_sentences.text_hi: ध्यान से देखिए — एक brighter dot आगे बढ़ता रहता है, magnet के अंदर से होकर, south से वापस north तक।
tts_sentences.text_te: జాగ్రత్తగా చూడండి — ఒక brighter dot ముందుకు సాగుతూనే ఉంటుంది, magnet లోపలి నుండి, south నుండి తిరిగి north వరకు.

tts_sentences.text_en: A magnetic field line never starts or ends — unlike an electric field line on a charge.
tts_sentences.text_hi: एक magnetic field line कभी शुरू या खत्म नहीं होती — charge वाली electric field line के विपरीत।
tts_sentences.text_te: ఒక magnetic field line ఎప్పుడూ మొదలవదు లేదా ముగియదు — charge పై ఉండే electric field line కి భిన్నంగా.

misconception_watch.belief: Magnetic field lines start at the north pole and end at the south pole, just like electric field lines on charges.
misconception_watch.visual_counter: A faint tracer riding the external arc appears to die out at the south pole — then a brighter tracer continues straight through the magnet, closing the loop back at the north pole.
misconception_watch.one_line_fix: Magnetic field lines never start or end; they are continuous closed loops that pass through the magnet from S to N inside.

STATE_3.title: No magnetic monopole — cut it and get two complete dipoles

scene_composition.text: Cut the bar magnet at its centre

The magnet splits into two halves that
separate from each other.

Brand-new N and S poles ignite right at
the cut — each half is instantly its own
complete magnet with a full field loop.
No magnetic monopole: every cut makes two complete dipoles, anywhere along the length

tts_sentences.text_en: Cut the bar magnet exactly at its centre, and the two halves begin to separate.
tts_sentences.text_hi: bar magnet को ठीक उसके centre पर काटिए, और दोनों halves अलग होना शुरू हो जाते हैं।
tts_sentences.text_te: bar magnet ను సరిగ్గా దాని centre వద్ద కట్ చేయండి, రెండు halves వేరవడం మొదలవుతుంది.

tts_sentences.text_en: New north and south poles ignite right at the cut — each half is instantly its own complete magnet.
tts_sentences.text_hi: cut पर बिल्कुल नए north और south poles जल उठते हैं — हर half तुरंत अपने आप में एक complete magnet है।
tts_sentences.text_te: cut వద్ద సరికొత్త north మరియు south poles వెలుగుతాయి — ప్రతి half వెంటనే తనలో తానే ఒక complete magnet.

tts_sentences.text_en: There is no magnetic monopole — cut it again anywhere else and you still get two full magnets.
tts_sentences.text_hi: कोई magnetic monopole नहीं होता — इसे कहीं और फिर से काटिए, फिर भी दो पूरे magnets मिलते हैं।
tts_sentences.text_te: magnetic monopole అనేది లేదు — దీన్ని మరెక్కడైనా మళ్ళీ కట్ చేసినా, ఇంకా రెండు పూర్తి magnets వస్తాయి.

misconception_watch.belief: If you cut a bar magnet in half you can isolate a single north pole and a single south pole.
misconception_watch.visual_counter: The magnet splits and brand-new N and S poles ignite at both cut faces, each half closing its own complete field loop — cutting again, anywhere along its length, always repeats the same result.
misconception_watch.one_line_fix: There is no magnetic monopole: every cut produces two complete dipoles, each with a north AND a south pole.

drill_downs: why can't you get just a north pole by cutting a magnet
what happens if you cut a magnet into many tiny pieces
does the new pole come from somewhere inside the magnet
is there such a thing as a magnetic monopole

STATE_4.title: The magnetic moment m — one arrow, locked S to N

scene_composition.text: One vector m captures the whole magnet

The magnet flips a half turn — the pole
ends swap places.

The moment arrow m still points from
the new S to the new N — it is locked
to the body, not to world direction.
Magnetic moment m: points S → N, magnitude m = N·I·A

tts_sentences.text_en: The whole magnet is captured by one vector, the magnetic moment m, pointing from south to north.
tts_sentences.text_hi: पूरे magnet को एक vector, magnetic moment m, दर्शाता है, जो south से north की ओर point करता है।
tts_sentences.text_te: మొత్తం magnet ను ఒక vector, magnetic moment m, సూచిస్తుంది, ఇది south నుండి north వైపు point చేస్తుంది.

tts_sentences.text_en: Now the magnet flips a half turn — notice the poles swap ends, but the arrow m still points from south to north.
tts_sentences.text_hi: अब magnet आधा turn flip होता है — देखिए poles के ends बदल जाते हैं, पर arrow m अब भी south से north की ओर point करता है।
tts_sentences.text_te: ఇప్పుడు magnet సగం turn flip అవుతుంది — poles యొక్క ends మారడం గమనించండి, కానీ arrow m ఇప్పటికీ south నుండి north వైపు point చేస్తుంది.

tts_sentences.text_en: The arrow is locked to the body: wherever south and north end up, m follows.
tts_sentences.text_hi: arrow body से locked रहता है: south और north चाहे कहीं भी हों, m उन्हें follow करता है।
tts_sentences.text_te: arrow body కి locked అయి ఉంటుంది: south మరియు north ఎక్కడ ఉన్నా, m వాటిని follow చేస్తుంది.

STATE_5.title: A bar magnet ≡ a solenoid (m = N·I·A)

scene_composition.text: A current-carrying solenoid replaces the magnet

A coil of many turns carrying current I
cross-fades in over the bar magnet.

The far field stays exactly the same the
whole time — the solenoid IS a magnet
of moment m = N·I·A.
Bar magnet ≡ solenoid: magnetism in matter comes from tiny current loops

tts_sentences.text_en: Recall a coil of many turns carrying current — its far field looks exactly like this magnet's field.
tts_sentences.text_hi: याद कीजिए, कई turns वाली एक coil जिसमें current बहता है — इसका far field बिल्कुल इस magnet के field जैसा दिखता है।
tts_sentences.text_te: గుర్తుచేసుకోండి, చాలా turns ఉన్న ఒక coil లో current ప్రవహిస్తుంది — దీని far field ఈ magnet field లాగే కనిపిస్తుంది.

tts_sentences.text_en: A bar magnet is equivalent to a solenoid of magnetic moment m equals N times I times A.
tts_sentences.text_hi: एक bar magnet, magnetic moment m equals N times I times A वाले solenoid के equivalent है।
tts_sentences.text_te: ఒక bar magnet, magnetic moment m equals N times I times A ఉన్న solenoid కి equivalent.

tts_sentences.text_en: So the magnetism of matter itself comes from countless tiny atomic current loops.
tts_sentences.text_hi: तो पदार्थ का magnetism भी अनगिनत tiny atomic current loops से आता है।
tts_sentences.text_te: కాబట్టి పదార్థం యొక్క magnetism కూడా లెక్కలేనన్ని tiny atomic current loops నుండి వస్తుంది.

drill_downs: why is a bar magnet like a current-carrying coil
does a permanent magnet actually have current flowing in it
what is n i a for a solenoid
where does a permanent magnet's magnetism actually come from

STATE_6.title: At equal r, axial field is TWICE the equatorial field

scene_composition.text: Same distance r — axial vs equatorial

A ghost pair of equal-length arrows
appears first, one axial, one equatorial,
both at the same distance r.

The real arrows snap in unequal: the
axial one is exactly twice as long as
the equatorial one, same r.
B_axial : B_equatorial = 2 : 1 at the same distance r

tts_sentences.text_en: Two ghost arrows of equal length appear first, at the same distance r — one axial, one equatorial.
tts_sentences.text_hi: पहले बराबर length के दो ghost arrows दिखते हैं, same distance r पर — एक axial, एक equatorial।
tts_sentences.text_te: మొదట సమాన length ఉన్న రెండు ghost arrows కనిపిస్తాయి, అదే distance r వద్ద — ఒకటి axial, ఒకటి equatorial.

tts_sentences.text_en: But the real field snaps in differently: the axial arrow is exactly twice as long as the equatorial one, same distance r.
tts_sentences.text_hi: पर real field अलग तरह से snap होता है: axial arrow, equatorial से ठीक दोगुना लंबा है, same distance r पर।
tts_sentences.text_te: కానీ real field వేరుగా snap అవుతుంది: axial arrow, equatorial కంటే సరిగ్గా రెట్టింపు పొడవు, అదే distance r వద్ద.

tts_sentences.text_en: A dipole field is direction-dependent — axial is always twice equatorial.
tts_sentences.text_hi: एक dipole field direction-dependent होता है — axial हमेशा equatorial से दोगुना होता है।
tts_sentences.text_te: ఒక dipole field direction-dependent — axial ఎల్లప్పుడూ equatorial కంటే రెట్టింపు.

misconception_watch.belief: At the same distance from a bar magnet, the field should be the same strength in every direction — axial and equatorial should be equal.
misconception_watch.visual_counter: A ghost pair of equal-length arrows fades in first — then the real arrows snap in showing the axial arrow twice as long as the equatorial one, at the exact same distance r.
misconception_watch.one_line_fix: At equal distance r, the axial field is exactly TWICE the equatorial field — a dipole field is direction-dependent.

STATE_7.title: The field falls as 1/r³ — steeper than a point charge

scene_composition.text: Sweep the distance r — how fast does B fall?

A dashed ghost pair, anchored at r = 2,
marks what a 1/r² guess would predict
as distance grows.

The real arrows shrink much faster —
by r = 4 the real field has dropped to
one eighth, not one quarter.
B_axial = (μ₀/4π)(2m/r³)   B_equatorial = (μ₀/4π)(m/r³)

tts_sentences.text_en: A dashed ghost pair, anchored at r equals 2, marks what a one-over-r-squared guess would predict as distance grows.
tts_sentences.text_hi: r equals 2 पर anchored एक dashed ghost pair दिखाती है कि distance बढ़ने पर one-over-r-squared guess क्या predict करता।
tts_sentences.text_te: r equals 2 వద్ద anchor అయిన ఒక dashed ghost pair, distance పెరిగే కొద్దీ one-over-r-squared guess ఏమి predict చేస్తుందో చూపిస్తుంది.

tts_sentences.text_en: Watch the real arrows shrink much faster as r sweeps 2 to 4 — the field falls as one over r cubed.
tts_sentences.text_hi: r के 2 से 4 तक sweep होने पर real arrows को तेज़ी से shrink होते देखिए — field one over r cubed के अनुसार गिरता है।
tts_sentences.text_te: r 2 నుండి 4 వరకు sweep అయ్యేటప్పుడు real arrows వేగంగా shrink అవడం చూడండి — field one over r cubed ప్రకారం తగ్గుతుంది.

tts_sentences.text_en: Double the distance and the real field drops to one eighth, not one quarter.
tts_sentences.text_hi: Distance दोगुना कीजिए और real field one eighth तक गिर जाता है, one quarter नहीं।
tts_sentences.text_te: Distance రెట్టింపు చేస్తే real field one eighth కి పడిపోతుంది, one quarter కాదు.

misconception_watch.belief: The magnetic field of a bar magnet should fall off as 1/r², the same way a point charge's field does.
misconception_watch.visual_counter: A dashed ghost pair anchored at r = 2 (matching a 1/r² guess) is left behind as the real arrows shrink faster; by r = 4 the real field has dropped to one eighth while the ghost guess has only dropped to one quarter.
misconception_watch.one_line_fix: A dipole field falls as 1/r³, steeper than a point charge's 1/r² — double the distance and the real field drops to one eighth.

drill_downs: why does the magnetic field fall off faster than 1/r squared
is the dipole field the same as coulomb's law falloff
why is the axial field twice the equatorial field at large r
what happens to the field if I double the distance from the magnet

STATE_8.title: The electrostatic analog — same field as an electric dipole

scene_composition.text: An electric dipole glides in beside the magnet

A translucent +q / −q electric dipole,
with its own field, glides toward the
bar magnet's field.

The two field patterns snap into exact
coincidence — the same shape, the same
1/r³ falloff.
m ↔ p     μ₀/4π ↔ 1/(4πε₀)

tts_sentences.text_en: Beside the magnet, a translucent electric dipole glides in with its own field.
tts_sentences.text_hi: magnet के बगल में, एक translucent electric dipole अपने field के साथ glide करते हुए आता है।
tts_sentences.text_te: magnet పక్కన, ఒక translucent electric dipole తన field తో glide అవుతూ వస్తుంది.

tts_sentences.text_en: Watch the two field patterns snap into exact coincidence: a bar magnet's field matches an electric dipole's field.
tts_sentences.text_hi: दोनों field patterns को बिल्कुल coincide होते देखिए: bar magnet का field, electric dipole के field से match करता है।
tts_sentences.text_te: రెండు field patterns ఖచ్చితంగా coincide అవడం చూడండి: bar magnet యొక్క field, electric dipole యొక్క field తో match అవుతుంది.

tts_sentences.text_en: The magnetic moment m plays the role of p, with mu zero over four pi replacing one over four pi epsilon zero.
tts_sentences.text_hi: Magnetic moment m, p की भूमिका निभाता है, जहाँ mu zero over four pi, one over four pi epsilon zero की जगह लेता है।
tts_sentences.text_te: Magnetic moment m, p పాత్రను పోషిస్తుంది, ఇక్కడ mu zero over four pi, one over four pi epsilon zero స్థానంలో ఉంటుంది.

STATE_9.title: Your turn — drag m and r

scene_composition.text: Explore: drag the moment m and the distance r

Watch the axial and equatorial probe
arrows respond live to both sliders.

Challenge: double r — by what factor
does the field drop? (1/r³ → ÷8)
B_axial ∝ 2m/r³ · B_equatorial ∝ m/r³ · ratio always 2 : 1

tts_sentences.text_en: Your turn — drag the moment m and the distance r, and watch both arrows respond live.
tts_sentences.text_hi: अब आपकी बारी — moment m और distance r को drag कीजिए, और दोनों arrows को live respond करते देखिए।
tts_sentences.text_te: ఇప్పుడు మీ వంతు — moment m మరియు distance r ను drag చేయండి, రెండు arrows live గా respond అవడం చూడండి.

STATE_1.label: Field lines loop, then a compass settles
STATE_1.caption: Field has a direction

STATE_2.label: One field line closes through the magnet
STATE_2.caption: Lines close, no ends

STATE_3.label: Cut the magnet: two new dipoles appear
STATE_3.caption: Cut it: two magnets

STATE_4.label: Moment m points S→N and rides the magnet
STATE_4.caption: One arrow: m, S→N

STATE_5.label: A bar magnet is a solenoid of current
STATE_5.caption: A magnet is current

STATE_6.label: Axial field is twice the equatorial field
STATE_6.caption: Axial is twice equatorial

STATE_8.label: A bar magnet mirrors an electric dipole
STATE_8.caption: Same field as p

STATE_9.label: Explore: drag moment m and distance r
STATE_9.caption: All yours

regeneration_variants.label: The compass needle
regeneration_variants.physical_world: The small magnetic compass needle from your geography or physics class
regeneration_variants.same_physics: The needle is a tiny bar magnet — a magnetic dipole with its own moment m — whose field is the same closed-loop dipole field, and which lines up tangent to any external field
regeneration_variants.scene_hint: A pivoted compass needle, red N end, with the same closed dipole field lines around it, settling along an external field.

regeneration_variants.label: Snapping a fridge magnet
regeneration_variants.physical_world: Breaking a fridge magnet or a flexible magnetic strip in half
regeneration_variants.same_physics: Each broken piece is instantly a complete dipole with a new N and S at the break — the no-monopole rule, identical to cutting a bar magnet
regeneration_variants.scene_hint: A magnetic strip snapping, each half lighting up new N and S poles at the fracture with its own field loops.

assessment.mastery_definition: A student has mastered this concept when they can explain that a bar magnet is a magnetic DIPOLE whose field lines are continuous CLOSED loops (out of N, around outside to S, and through the magnet from S back to N), so a line never starts or ends — unlike electric field lines, which begin on positive charge and end on negative; that there is NO magnetic monopole, so cutting a magnet always yields two complete dipoles with new poles appearing at the cut; that the magnetic moment m points from S to N with magnitude m = NIA, the bar magnet being EQUIVALENT to a solenoid of that moment (magnetism in matter arising from tiny atomic current loops); that the dipole far field falls as 1/r³ with the axial field exactly TWICE the equatorial field at the same distance (B_axial = (μ₀/4π)(2m/r³), B_equatorial = (μ₀/4π)(m/r³)); and that this is the exact electrostatic analog of an electric dipole, with m ↔ p and μ₀/4π ↔ 1/4πε₀.

questions.stem: How do a bar magnet's field lines differ from an electric dipole's field lines?

options.A: Magnetic field lines are continuous closed loops (no start or end); electric field lines start on + charge and end on − charge
options.B: Magnetic field lines start at N and end at S, exactly like electric field lines on charges
options.C: Magnetic field lines are straight; electric field lines are curved
options.D: There is no difference — both are closed loops

distractor_misconceptions.B: Treats N and S as field-line sources/sinks like + and − charges, missing that magnetic lines close through the magnet
distractor_misconceptions.C: Confuses field-line shape with the real distinction (open vs closed)
distractor_misconceptions.D: Misses that electric field lines are open (start/end on charge), not closed

questions.tested_idea: Magnetic field lines are continuous closed loops; electric field lines start and end on charges.
questions.parallel_form_stem: Trace a magnetic field line of a bar magnet from the north pole. Describe where it goes and whether it ever ends, and contrast with an electric field line.

questions.stem: You cut a bar magnet exactly in half at its middle. What do you get?

options.A: Two complete magnets, each with its own N and S — new poles appear at the cut
options.B: One isolated north pole and one isolated south pole
options.C: Two pieces that are each unmagnetised
options.D: One magnet and one non-magnetic piece

distractor_misconceptions.B: Believes a single pole (a monopole) can be isolated by cutting
distractor_misconceptions.C: Thinks cutting destroys the magnetism rather than creating two dipoles
distractor_misconceptions.D: Thinks only one half keeps the magnetism

questions.tested_idea: No magnetic monopole exists: cutting a magnet yields two complete dipoles.
questions.parallel_form_stem: A long bar magnet is broken into three pieces. How many poles does each piece have, and why can you never obtain a single isolated pole?

questions.stem: In which direction does the magnetic moment m of a bar magnet point, and what sets its magnitude?

options.A: From S to N along the axis, with magnitude m = NIA (the equivalent solenoid)
options.B: From N to S along the axis, like a field line outside the magnet
options.C: Perpendicular to the axis, in the direction of the equatorial field
options.D: It has no definite direction; only its magnitude matters

distractor_misconceptions.B: Reverses the moment to N→S, confusing it with the external field-line direction
distractor_misconceptions.C: Confuses the moment's axis with the equatorial field direction
distractor_misconceptions.D: Treats the moment as a scalar, missing that it is a vector S→N

questions.tested_idea: The magnetic moment m points S→N with magnitude m = NIA.
questions.parallel_form_stem: Define the magnetic moment of a bar magnet: state its direction relative to the poles and the formula that gives its magnitude via the equivalent solenoid.

questions.stem: A bar magnet is said to be equivalent to what, and why does that matter?

options.A: A solenoid of moment m = NIA producing the same far field — magnetism in matter comes from tiny current loops
options.B: A single point charge, since both have a 1/r² field
options.C: A pair of isolated monopoles held a fixed distance apart
options.D: A straight current-carrying wire, since both involve current

distractor_misconceptions.B: Confuses a dipole (1/r³) with a point charge (1/r²)
distractor_misconceptions.C: Reintroduces monopoles, which do not exist
distractor_misconceptions.D: Picks a wire (1/r field, not a dipole) instead of a solenoid

questions.tested_idea: A bar magnet is equivalent to a solenoid of moment m = NIA; magnetism arises from current loops.
questions.parallel_form_stem: Explain in what sense a bar magnet and a current-carrying solenoid are equivalent, and what this says about the origin of magnetism in matter.

questions.stem: At the same distance r from a bar magnet, how does the axial field compare to the equatorial field, and how do they depend on r?

options.B: They are equal, and both fall as 1/r²

distractor_misconceptions.B: Assumes the field is isotropic and uses a point-charge 1/r² falloff
distractor_misconceptions.C: Inverts the ratio, making equatorial the stronger one
distractor_misconceptions.D: Gets the 2:1 ratio but uses the wrong (point-charge) 1/r² falloff

questions.tested_idea: B_axial = 2·B_equatorial at equal r, and both fall as 1/r³ (a dipole field).
questions.parallel_form_stem: Two points are equally distant from a short bar magnet, one on its axis and one on its perpendicular bisector. Compare the magnetic field magnitudes and state how each scales with distance.

questions.stem: What is the electrostatic analog of a bar magnet, and how do the quantities correspond?

options.A: An electric dipole: the magnetic moment m plays the role of p, and μ₀/4π replaces 1/4πε₀
options.B: A single point charge, with m playing the role of the charge q
options.C: A uniformly charged sphere, with m playing the role of the total charge
options.D: There is no analog — magnetism is unrelated to electrostatics

distractor_misconceptions.B: Maps the magnet to a monopole/point charge instead of a dipole
distractor_misconceptions.C: Chooses a charge distribution with a different (non-dipole) field

questions.tested_idea: A bar magnet is the magnetic analog of an electric dipole (m ↔ p, μ₀/4π ↔ 1/4πε₀).
questions.parallel_form_stem: State the electrostatic analog of a bar magnet and give the correspondence between the magnetic and electric quantities in the far-field dipole formulas.
